response: create_tasks with task_name='Call the dentist', defer_date='2026-03-16', due_date='2026-03-20'. Defer date hides the task until Monday March 16; due date sets the deadline for the Friday appointment.

1. create_projects with name='Kitchen Renovation', project_type='sequential'. 2. create_tasks with 5 items in order, all with project_id set to the new project: [{task_name:'Get quotes'}, {task_name:'Pick contractor'}, {task_name:'Sign contract'}, {task_name:'Demolition'}, {task_name:'Installation'}]. In a sequential project, order = dependencies, so only the first task will be available.
get_tasks(available_only=True). This returns only tasks that are currently actionable (not blocked, deferred, or in completed/dropped containers).

update_tasks with items: [{id:'task-001', flagged:true}, {id:'task-002', flagged:true}, {id:'task-003', flagged:true}]. Flagging marks them for today's focus. The user can then view flagged tasks via get_tasks(flagged_only=True).

update_tasks with items: [{id:'task-123', task_name:'Buy groceries'}, {id:'task-456', flagged:true}, {id:'task-789', flagged:true}]. Each item specifies only the fields to change; task_name for the rename, flagged for the other two.
update_tasks with items: [{id:'task-100', project_id:'proj-200'}]. Setting project_id moves the task from inbox to the specified project.
update_projects with items: [{id:'proj-888', status:'dropped'}]. Dropping preserves the project in records but marks it as abandoned. Do NOT use delete_projects, which permanently removes it.
reorder_task(task_id='task-B', before_task_id='task-A'). This places task-B before task-A. In a sequential project, order determines dependencies, so task-B will now be available first.

Two calls: 1. create_tasks with items: [{task_name:'Review PR', tags:['Computer','Work']}]. Tags are set at creation via the tags parameter. 2. update_tasks with items: [{id:'task-555', add_tags:['Urgent']}]. Use add_tags (not tags) to append without replacing existing tags.

update_tasks with items: [{id:'task-300', due_date:''}]. Empty string clears the date. Note: if the task's project has a due date, the effective due date will still show the project's date (inherited), but the task's own due date is removed.

create_tasks with items: [{task_name:'Buy nails', parent_task_id:'task-400'}]. Use parent_task_id to create a subtask. Do NOT also specify project_id — parent_task_id and project_id are mutually exclusive.
get_tasks(tag_filter=['Errands','Weekend']). The default tag_filter_mode is 'and', so this returns tasks with BOTH tags.

Multi-step planning: 1. get_tasks(flagged_only=True) — see what's already flagged for focus. 2. get_tasks(overdue=True) — find overdue items needing attention. 3. get_tasks(available_only=True) — see all actionable tasks. Review results and recommend priorities. Optionally switch_perspective('Forecast') to show the day view.

1. create_projects with items: [{name:'Website Redesign', folder_path:'Work', project_type:'sequential', review_interval_weeks:2}]. 2. Using the returned project ID, create_tasks with 6 items in order: [{task_name:'Wireframes', project_id:PROJECT_ID, tags:['Web Team']}, {task_name:'Design mockups', project_id:PROJECT_ID, tags:['Web Team']}, {task_name:'Frontend build', project_id:PROJECT_ID, tags:['Web Team']}, {task_name:'Backend API', project_id:PROJECT_ID, tags:['Web Team']}, {task_name:'QA testing', project_id:PROJECT_ID, tags:['Web Team']}, {task_name:'Launch', project_id:PROJECT_ID, tags:['Web Team']}]. The 'Web Team' tag must already exist. Sequential project means tasks are done in order.

get_projects() — returns all projects with nextReviewDate and lastReviewDate fields. Filter the results for projects where nextReviewDate is in the past (before today's date). There is no direct 'overdue_review' filter parameter, so client-side filtering is needed.
update_projects with items: [{id:'proj-777', status:'done'}]. This marks the project as completed. Do NOT use status='dropped' — that means abandoned, not finished.

set_focus does NOT support tasks — it only accepts projects or folders. Explain this limitation to the user. Alternative: use get_tasks(task_id='task-999') to retrieve the task, or flag it with update_tasks([{id:'task-999', flagged:true}]) and use get_tasks(flagged_only=True) to see only flagged items.

1. get_tasks(inbox_only=True) to retrieve all inbox tasks and their IDs. 2. update_tasks with each task's ID and completed:true, e.g. [{id:'task-X', completed:true}, {id:'task-Y', completed:true}, ...]. Using completed=True ensures recurring tasks spawn their next occurrence correctly.

No tool call needed — this is an interpretation question. The task is an action group (it has subtaskCount:3 and sequential:true). A parent task with subtasks shows blocked:true while its subtasks are active — this is normal behavior, not an error. The server instructions explicitly state: 'Parent shows blocked: true while subtasks are active — this is normal, not an error. Do not try to unblock it.' The user should work on the subtasks instead.

No tool call needed — this is an explanation question. Yes, that's correct. The 'next' field means the first available action in a sequential project/action group. In parallel projects, all incomplete tasks are available simultaneously, so all have next:true. In sequential projects, only the first incomplete task has next:true because subsequent tasks are blocked until it's completed.

No tool call needed — this is a clarification about inherited dates. Per the server instructions: 'Dates returned by get_tasks are always effective (inherited). A task with no direct due date WILL show its project's due date in the dueDate field — this is correct behavior, not a bug.' If the tasks are showing empty dueDate, this contradicts the stated behavior. The user should re-run get_tasks(project_id='proj-q2') and check the dueDate fields — they should show the inherited April 15 date. If tasks truly show empty, it may be a project-level issue worth investigating.

No tool call needed — this is an explanation question. They may not be the same type. Check the projectType field from get_projects. 'parallel' means tasks can be done in any order (like Home Repairs). 'single_actions' is a grab-bag with no completion goal — it cannot auto-complete when children finish (like Errands). Both show sequential=false (the deprecated field), but projectType distinguishes them. Use get_projects(project_id=...) on each to check their projectType.

No tool call needed — this is an explanation based on the server instructions. Using update_tasks with completed:true on a recurring task WILL create the next occurrence. The instructions state: 'completed=True uses mark complete, which creates the next occurrence. This is guaranteed.' So the recurrence is preserved and the next occurrence will be spawned.

update_tasks or create_tasks with planned_date='2026-03-18', due_date='2026-03-20'. Planned date is a scheduling signal only — it doesn't hide the task or affect availability. The task remains available before Wednesday. Defer date would hide it until that date, which the user doesn't want. Due date sets the Friday deadline.

create_tasks with items: [{task_name:'Prepare presentation', defer_date:'2026-03-16', planned_date:'2026-03-18', due_date:'2026-03-20'}]. Defer date (Monday) hides it until materials arrive. Planned date (Wednesday) is when they plan to work on it. Due date (Friday) is the hard deadline.
update_tasks with items: [{id:'task-600', planned_date:''}]. Empty string clears the planned date.

No tool call needed. The instructions say to use repeatSummary for display, not parse the RRULE. The answer is right in the data: 'Every week on Mon, Wed, Fri'. The task repeats three times a week on Monday, Wednesday, and Friday, on a fixed schedule (repetitionMethod: 'fixed').
update_tasks with items: [{id:'task-810', recurrence:'FREQ=WEEKLY;INTERVAL=2'}]. This changes the recurrence rule from every 1 week to every 2 weeks.

No tool call needed — explanation question. With repetitionMethod 'due_after_completion', the next due date is calculated relative to when you completed it, not the original schedule. So if you completed it today, the next occurrence will be due one week from today (not next Monday on a fixed schedule).
update_tasks with items: [{id:'task-900', recurrence:''}]. Setting recurrence to empty string clears the recurrence, making it a one-time task.

update_tasks with items: [{id:'task-820', recurrence:'FREQ=DAILY;INTERVAL=1', repetition_method:'due_after_completion'}]. FREQ=DAILY sets daily repetition; repetition_method='due_after_completion' means the next due date shifts based on actual completion date.
update_tasks with items: [{id:'task-830', recurrence:'FREQ=DAILY;INTERVAL=1', repetition_method:'fixed'}]. This adds a daily recurrence on a fixed schedule to the existing task.

update_tags with items: [{id:'tag-050', status:'dropped'}]. Per the server instructions, a dropped tag is 'hidden from picker' but 'does NOT affect task availability'. So tasks with this tag remain available and actionable.

update_tags with items: [{id:'tag-052', status:'active'}]. This makes 'Waiting' active again so tasks with it are available. No action needed for 'Archive' (tag-053) — it's already dropped and should stay that way. Note: when 'Waiting' was 'on hold', tasks with only that tag were excluded from the Available perspective.

create_projects with items: [{name:'Someday/Maybe', project_type:'single_actions'}]. A single_actions project has no completion goal and cannot auto-complete when items are checked off — it's designed as a grab-bag list.
update_folders with items: [{id:'folder-999', status:'dropped'}]. Setting status to 'dropped' hides the folder from view without deleting it, effectively archiving it.
